page.content: The data table presented provides a comprehensive overview of the various types of permits for advanced treatment technologies employed in waterworks from 2007 to 2012. This period marks a significant evolution in the management of treated water, particularly as municipalities increasingly adopted innovative treatment methods to address growing environmental and public health concerns.

cells.cell_text: Type of Permit for Advanced Treatment
addition of sodium hydroxide, precipitation by adding iron(II), flocculation with polyaluminium chloride
addition of iron chloride, iron sulphate or adsorption filters with iron oxide

page.content:
Notably, the column "Treated Water (million m³/year)" highlights the substantial volumes of water processed, reflecting the critical role of advanced treatment in ensuring safe drinking water supplies. 

The "Active Waterworks 2012" column further emphasizes the operational landscape of these facilities, indicating how many plants were actively utilizing advanced treatment methodologies at that time. Examples of treatment methods, such as the addition of sodium hydroxide and precipitation through iron(II) compounds, demonstrate the diverse approaches taken to enhance water quality. Additionally, the inclusion of flocculation with polyaluminium chloride illustrates the variety of techniques applied to optimize treatment efficiency. As the data indicate, the effectiveness of these methods is crucial, with metrics such as Nb/Nc ratios and specific gene sets providing insights into the biological implications of treatment processes. Together, these elements underscore the importance of regulatory frameworks in guiding the deployment of advanced treatment technologies for sustainable water management.
